theory.intro: Prisms and pyramids are both named after their base, but they are built differently. A prism has a **uniform cross-section**; a pyramid comes to a point.
facts: A **prism** has **two identical, parallel bases** and the same shape all the way through (uniform cross-section).
A **pyramid** has **one base** and its side faces are triangles that meet at a single point, the **apex**.
Prism side faces are **rectangles**; pyramid side faces are **triangles**.
Slice a prism parallel to its base and every slice is the same shape; a pyramid's slices shrink towards the apex.
steps: Count the bases: two matching ends → prism; one base → pyramid.
Look for an apex (a single top point): yes → pyramid.
Check the side faces: rectangles → prism; triangles → pyramid.

foundation.q: Is this solid a prism or a pyramid?
Is this solid a prism or a pyramid?
How many bases does a prism have?
How many bases does a pyramid have?
What shape are the side faces of a prism?
What shape are the side faces of a pyramid?
Which solid has an apex (a single point on top): a prism or a pyramid?
Is this solid a prism or a pyramid?
Is this box-shaped solid a prism or a pyramid?
Which solid has the same cross-section all the way through: a prism or a pyramid?

development.q: A solid has $2$ identical triangular ends joined by $3$ rectangles. Prism or pyramid?
A solid has $1$ square base and $4$ triangular faces meeting at a point. Prism or pyramid?
If you slice a prism parallel to its base, what shape is each slice?
development.a: the same shape as the base

development.q: If you slice a pyramid parallel to its base moving upward, what happens to the slices?
development.a: they get smaller towards the apex

development.q: State one similarity and one difference between a prism and a pyramid.
development.a: Both named by their base; prism has 2 bases, pyramid has 1 (and an apex)

development.q: A solid has $6$ faces, all rectangles, with two matching ends. Prism or pyramid?
Which has more faces meeting at its top vertex: a prism or a pyramid? Explain.
development.a: a pyramid — all triangular faces meet at the apex

development.q: A solid has $5$ faces: $2$ triangles and $3$ rectangles. Prism or pyramid, and why?
development.a: prism — rectangular side faces, two triangle ends

development.q: A solid has $5$ faces: $1$ square and $4$ triangles. Prism or pyramid, and why?
development.a: pyramid — one base, triangular faces to an apex

development.q: Compare the two solids shown: which is the prism?

mastery.q: A solid has a uniform pentagonal cross-section. Is it a prism or pyramid, and how many side faces?
A pyramid and a prism share the same square base. Which has more faces, and by how many?
True or false: every slice parallel to the base of a triangular pyramid is the same size. Explain.
mastery.a: False — slices shrink towards the apex

mastery.q: A solid has $2$ hexagonal faces and $6$ rectangular faces. Classify it and explain why it is not a pyramid.
mastery.a: hexagonal prism — it has 2 bases, no apex

mastery.q: Sort these by type: triangular prism, square pyramid, cube, pentagonal pyramid. Which are prisms?
A prism and a pyramid both have $5$ faces. What base does each have?
Why is a cube a prism but not a pyramid?
mastery.a: It has two identical parallel square bases and no apex

mastery.q: A solid's faces are $1$ rectangle and $4$ triangles. Classify it.
Explain why a pyramid's volume is less than a prism with the same base and height.
mastery.a: The pyramid narrows to a point, so it encloses less space

mastery.q: A net has $1$ pentagon and $5$ triangles. Will it fold into a prism or pyramid? Name it.
